candidates.title: Wildberger (2008) — Pell's Equation Without Irrational Numbers
candidates.body: N. J. Wildberger. arXiv:0806.2490v2. Generates an infinite number of integer solutions to Pell's equation x² − Dy² = 1 using only basic matrix arithmetic, without irrational numbers or continued fractions. Worked examples for D = 2, 7, 61. Connects to Stern-Brocot tree and universal geometry. PDF at Documents/wildberger_corpus/pell_no_irrationals.pdf.

--- candidate_02 ---
Title: Philomath p.16: digital root / mod-9 definition
Body: The digital root, also known as modular math, is one important branch of number theory... In simple words, what the digital root operation does is to reduce a number down to a single digit. This is done by continuously summing the number's individual digits.

--- candidate_03 ---
Title: Philomath p.29-30: primes p>3 avoid D-roots {3,6,9}
Body: In the D-space, with the exception of number 3, the digital root of any known prime number is anything but 3, 6, or 9... no prime number can have a digital root of 3, 6, or 9.

--- candidate_04 ---
Title: Wildberger (2008) — Chromogeometry
Body: N. J. Wildberger. arXiv:0806.3617v1. Three-fold symmetric geometry combining Euclidean (blue) and two relativistic (red, green) planar metrical systems. Each geometry has its own Euler line, nine-point circle, and orthocenter; they interact via algebraic identities. Transcends Klein's Erlangen program. PDF at Documents/wildberger_corpus/chromogeometry_2008.pdf.

Body: N. J. Wildberger, UNSW. Introduces the 'Mutation Game' on directed multigraphs (Martians vs anti-Martians mutating under graph-local rules). Root populations under the mutation dynamics correspond to Coxeter-graph structure. Grounds [215] qa_graph_cayley_wildberger_mutation cert family. Text extract at corpus/wildberger_text/mutation_game_2020.txt.

--- candidate_08 ---
Title: Wildberger 2008 Chromogeometry: blue/red/green 3-fold planar metrical trio
Body: Chromogeometry brings together Euclidean geometry (called blue) and two relativistic geometries (called red and green), in a surprising three-fold symmetry. We show how the red and green 'Euler lines' and 'nine-point circles' of a triangle interact with the usual blue ones, and how the three orthocenters form an associated triangle with interesting collinearities.

--- candidate_09 ---
Title: Philomath p.19: 9 as additive identity of D-space (QA Singularity orbit)
Body: Even though number 9 is the last and largest number of the D-space, it behaves in this finite space as number 0 does in the regular infinite numerical space. For example, adding 9 to any number will not change its digital root... This property of number 9, being the largest and smallest at the same time, is unique to the D-space.
